1: Moses's summons to "hear, O Israel" — the command to attend to the covenant address — is the posture Christ calls from his audience as well (Matthew 13:43; Mark 7:14: "let him who has ears to hear, hear"). Both Mosaic and Messianic addresses demand active reception. The gathered community at the Plains of Moab is the type of the church assembled to hear the word of its new covenant Lord.
2: The Sinai covenant established the pattern of YHWH binding himself to a people by sovereign oath. Christ inaugurates the new covenant by his blood (Luke 22:20; 1 Corinthians 11:25), superior to Sinai because it rests on better promises (Hebrews 8:6) and has a better mediator (Hebrews 8:1–2). The repeated YHWH-covenant formula here is the prototype of which the new covenant is the fulfillment.
3: "Not with our fathers... but with us — all of us here and alive today." Moses insists the covenant is present-tense and contemporary, not merely historical. The new covenant has the same urgency: "today, if you hear his voice, do not harden your hearts" (Hebrews 3:7–8, 15). Christ addresses the church directly across time (Revelation 3:20): always contemporary, always now.
4: "Face to face at the mountain, out of the midst of the fire" — the Sinai encounter was as direct as Moses could mediate, yet still through cloud and fire. Christ is the definitive face-to-face: "whoever has seen me has seen the Father" (John 14:9); "the Word became flesh and dwelt among us" (John 1:14). Hebrews 1:3 calls Christ "the exact imprint of his nature" — the full face of God, not filtered through fire or cloud.
5: Moses stood "between the LORD and you" — the mediatorial position at Sinai points directly to Christ as the one mediator between God and humanity (1 Timothy 2:5). But the contrast is sharp: Moses mediated because the people were afraid; Christ mediates as the Son, with unmediated access to the Father (John 10:30; Deuteronomy 17:21–22). Moses's mediation was a placeholder; Christ's is permanent and final (Hebrews 7:25).
6: "I am the LORD your God who brought you out of Egypt, out of the house of slavery" — the covenant prologue. The Ten Commandments are grounded not in moral obligation alone but in prior redemption: YHWH already acted; the law is the response of the redeemed, not the condition for redemption. Paul applies the same logic in 1 Corinthians 6:20: "you were bought with a price; therefore glorify God." Christ's death and resurrection (Luke 9:31 calls it his exodos) is the new prologue to the new covenant.
7: "You shall have no other gods before me." The first command forbids divided allegiance. Christ claims the same exclusive loyalty: "no one comes to the Father except through me" (John 14:6); "no one can serve two masters" (Matthew 6:24). Colossians 1:15–18 identifies Christ as the image of the invisible God in whom "all the fullness of God was pleased to dwell" (Colossians 1:19) — not one option among many but the singular self-expression of the divine.
8: "You shall not make for yourself a carved image." The prohibition of images is grounded in YHWH's uniqueness and refusal to be domesticated by human craft. Paradoxically, Christ himself is "the image of the invisible God" (Colossians 1:15; 2 Corinthians 4:4) — the one true image YHWH authorized, not carved but incarnate. The second commandment forbids human-made approximations because the genuine Image was already purposed: Christ as the last Adam restoring the image distorted in the fall (1 Corinthians 15:45–49).
9: "A jealous God, visiting the iniquity of the fathers on the children to the third and fourth generation." YHWH's jealousy is covenantal possessiveness — he will not share ultimate loyalty. Christ claims the same jealousy for his church: "I am jealous for you with a godly jealousy" (2 Corinthians 11:2). The consequence of covenant violation across generations is broken by Christ, who bore the accumulated iniquity of all generations (Isaiah 53:5–6; 1 Peter 2:24).
10: "Showing steadfast love to thousands of those who love me and keep my commandments" — the asymmetry is built in: four generations of punishment vs. thousands of generations of hesed. Paul extends this in Romans 5:20: "where sin increased, grace abounded all the more." Christ embodies that asymmetry — "while we were still sinners, Christ died for us" (Romans 5:8); grace vastly outweighs judgment in the economy of God.
11: "You shall not take the name of the LORD your God in vain." Philippians 2:9–11 — "God has given him the name above every name, so that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow." The reverence commanded for YHWH's name is now expressed through reverence for Christ's name, in whom the full character and presence of God is contained. The third commandment's honor is fulfilled in the exaltation of the name of Jesus.
12: "Observe the Sabbath day, to keep it holy." Hebrews 4:1–11 provides the fullest NT commentary: the Sabbath rest points forward to the eschatological rest that Christ provides. "There remains a Sabbath rest for the people of God, for whoever has entered God's rest has also rested from his works" (Hebrews 4:9–10). Christ is the rest-giver: "come to me, all who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest" (Matthew 11:28–29).
13: "Six days you shall labor and do all your work" — the six days of human labor echo the creation week (Gen 1–Deuteronomy 2:3). Work within this structure is dignified, not punishment. Christ's own labor — "the work the Father gave him to accomplish" (John 17:4; Deuteronomy 4:34) — is both the template and its completion: on the cross he declares "it is finished" (John 19:30), the divine work complete in a way that invites human rest.
14: The Sabbath rest extends to every member of the household, including servants and animals — a radical inclusion. The rest Christ offers is equally universal: "come to me, all who are weary" (Matthew 11:28) — no category of person excluded. The scope of Sabbath rest is the type of the scope of gospel rest; both are all-encompassing rather than elite or earned.
15: "Remember that you were a slave in the land of Egypt, and the LORD your God brought you out." The Exodus is the theological ground for Sabbath observance. Paul and the NT ground the new covenant's Sabbath-principle in the new exodus: Christ's death is the definitive mighty hand by which believers are brought out of slavery to sin and death (Colossians 1:13–14). The weekly rest is a memorial of liberation pointing to the eternal liberation Christ accomplishes.
16: "Honor your father and your mother" — the fifth commandment bridges the two tables of the law. Christ honored his Father perfectly (John 8:29: "I always do what pleases him") and also honored Mary at the cross (John 19:26–27). The command is fulfilled in his life from its first table (love of the Father) and its second (love of neighbor, including parents). His filial obedience is both example and enabling grace for his disciples.
17: "You shall not murder." Jesus deepens this to anger and contempt (Matthew 5:21–22), showing the command protects the image of God in every person. Christ is "the Prince of Life" (Acts 3:15) — the one who conquers death rather than inflicting it. His death as the one innocent person killed is the ultimate fulfillment of the command's concern: the one who should not have been killed absorbs the consequence so that those who deserve death may live (Romans 5:6–8).
18: "You shall not commit adultery." Jesus extends this to the heart (Matthew 5:27–28). Christ is the faithful bridegroom (John 3:29; Revelation 19:7–9; Ephesians 5:25–27) whose covenant faithfulness to his bride is absolute. Paul makes the type explicit: the marriage covenant is "a profound mystery" referring to Christ and the church (Ephesians 5:31–32). The marital fidelity commanded here is a type of Christ's covenant faithfulness to his people.
19: "You shall not steal." Christ came not to steal, kill, and destroy but that his people might have life abundantly (John 10:10). In Philippians 2:6–7 he "did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped" — a divine refusal to seize what was not given — and instead poured himself out. The anti-theft command is not merely honored in Christ's teaching but embodied in the very pattern of his self-giving incarnation.
20: "You shall not give false testimony against your neighbor." Christ is "the Truth" (John 14:6) and "the faithful and true witness" (Revelation 3:14; Deuteronomy 19:11) — he cannot bear false testimony because he is the very standard of truth. False witnesses were used against him at his trial (Matthew 26:59–61); his faithfulness to truth even when silence would have been safer (John 18:37: "for this purpose I was born, to bear witness to the truth") is the lived fulfillment of this command.
21: "You shall not covet your neighbor's wife... or anything that belongs to your neighbor." Paul identifies covetousness as idolatry (Colossians 3:5) — placing ultimate value on created things rather than on God. Christ is the supreme anti-covetousness model: "though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor, so that you by his poverty might become rich" (2 Corinthians 8:9). The covetous impulse is the distortion of desire; Christ redirects it toward the true treasure (Matthew 6:20).
22: "He wrote them on two tablets of stone and gave them to me." The stone tablets represent the externality of the Mosaic covenant. Paul's contrast in 2 Corinthians 3:2–3 is direct: the new covenant writes not on stone but by the Spirit on "tablets of human hearts." Jeremiah 31:33 prophesied this internal writing; Christ is the mediator of the new covenant that makes it possible (Hebrews 8:6–12). The tablets are the type; the Spirit-written heart is the antitype.
23: The tribal heads coming near to Moses while the people stayed back reflects Israel's mediated access to the divine word. In Christ, the veil is torn (Matthew 27:51): direct access to God through him is open to all. "Let us therefore draw near with confidence to the throne of grace" (Hebrews 4:16). The hierarchical access of Sinai is replaced by universal access through the one mediator Christ Jesus (1 Timothy 2:5).
24: "Today we have seen that God speaks with man, and man still lives" — the wonder that divine speech did not destroy its human recipients. The Incarnation is the definitive resolution: God speaks in the person of his Son (Hebrews 1:1–2), and this speech takes human form without destroying it. "The Word became flesh" (John 1:14) is the ultimate answer — the divine presence not filtered through fire but born in Bethlehem.
25: "But now why should we die? For this great fire will consume us." Israel's dread of direct divine encounter was well-founded: the holy God in unmediated presence would destroy sinful humanity. Christ removes this terror: Hebrews 12:18–24 contrasts the fire and darkness of Sinai with the joy of Mount Zion, "and to Jesus, the mediator of a new covenant." Through Christ's atoning work, the consuming fire has been met and satisfied on behalf of his people.
26: "Who among all flesh has ever heard the voice of the living God speaking from the midst of fire, as we have, and lived?" The implied answer: almost no one, yet Israel survived. In Christ, encounter with the living God is not only survivable but life-giving. Jesus says "my sheep hear my voice" (John 10:27): the terrifying voice from the fire has become the shepherd's voice that draws rather than destroys, guides rather than consumes.
27: "You speak to us all that the LORD our God speaks to you. We will listen and do it." The people voluntarily delegate their hearing to Moses. The NT applies the same structure to Christ: "in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son" (Hebrews 1:2). The church receives the divine word through the one mediator who is not only the channel but the content of what is spoken — he himself is the Word (John 1:1).
28: YHWH commends the people's proposal: "they are right in all that they have said." The divine affirmation of mediated relationship shows that YHWH himself arranges for such access where direct encounter is impossible. The supremacy of Christ as mediator is not a concession to weakness but a divine design: the Father has given all judgment and all access into the Son's hands (John 3:35; Deuteronomy 5:22).
29: "If only they had such a heart as this always, to fear me and keep all my commandments!" — the divine lament over the heart-incapacity of the Mosaic era. This longing is answered in the new covenant: "I will give them a new heart, and a new spirit I will put within them" (Ezekiel 36:26); "I will write my law on their hearts" (Jeremiah 31:33). The Spirit of Christ produces in believers the heart-obedience that the law demanded but could not generate (Romans 8:4; Galatians 5:22–23).
30: "Go, tell them: Return to your tents." The dismissal of the people while Moses remains in the divine presence is a pattern of Mosaic mediation — the mediator stays when the people cannot. Christ's ascension to the right hand of the Father (Acts 2:33; Hebrews 1:3) is the permanent fulfillment: he is always before the Father, always interceding (Hebrews 7:25), never dismissed or sent away from the divine presence.
31: "As for you, stand here with me, and I will tell you the whole commandment." Moses alone receives the full revelation — what he received in parts across time, Christ is simultaneously and entirely: "in him all the fullness of deity dwells bodily" (Colossians 2:9). John 1:18 — "the only Son, who is at the Father's side, he has made him known" — states that Christ has revealed what no other mediator could, precisely because he is himself the content of the revelation.
32: "You shall not turn aside to the right or to the left" — the image of the straight path. Jesus identifies himself as "the way" (John 14:6), not merely a direction-indicator but the path itself. The Deuteronomic "keep the way" finds its fulfillment in union with Christ: those who abide in him (John 15:4) cannot ultimately deviate, because the way is a person who holds rather than merely points.
33: "Walk in all the way that the LORD your God has commanded you, so that you may live." Life as the result of walking in the way — this is the promise animating the Mosaic system. Jesus claims to be both the way and the life (John 14:6): he does not merely point to life at the end of the path but is the life those who are in him already possess. "I give them eternal life and they shall never perish" (John 10:28).
